text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY OFFICE VISIT NOTE
Attending Physician: Medical Oncology
Visit Type: Established Patient Follow-Up — Metastatic Prostate Cancer

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old Polish-American male with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC) originally diagnosed approximately 24 months ago, now presenting for scheduled follow-up. He was seen today for evaluation of rising PSA values over the past three measurement intervals, which prompted this more urgent interval assessment. He reports new onset of low back pain over the past 4-6 weeks.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was diagnosed two years ago with de novo Stage IVB prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, presenting with a PSA of 187.4 ng/mL, clinical T3bN1M1b disease with bony metastases at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala, as well as enlarged right external iliac lymph nodes (up to 1.8 cm). He was initiated on androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months, combined with abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally daily plus prednisone 5 mg twice daily, consistent with an intensified regimen for mHSPC.

His initial response was excellent — PSA fell from 187.4 to 4.2 ng/mL at 3 months (97.8% decline), reaching a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at approximately 12 months. Testosterone has been maintained in the castrate range throughout (consistently <10 ng/dL). Bone scan at 18 months showed sclerotic changes at the known lesions without new lesions, and CT showed reduction in the right external iliac lymph nodes from 1.8 cm to 0.9 cm — an excellent imaging response to intensified hormonal therapy.

However, beginning at approximately 18 months, PSA began to rise from the nadir of 0.8, reaching 1.1 ng/mL at the time of the most recent bone scan. Subsequent PSA values have now been: 1.1 → 2.4 → 5.3 → 8.6 ng/mL over the past 6 months, representing three consecutive rises above the nadir with a calculated PSA doubling time of approximately 3.5 months. Testosterone was rechecked most recently and confirmed at <10 ng/dL, ruling out inadequate castration as the explanation for PSA rise.

Mr. Kowalski reports new low back pain over the past 4 to 6 weeks, which he describes as a dull aching in the lumbar region, rated 3/10 in intensity at rest and up to 5/10 with prolonged standing. He denies any radicular symptoms, saddle anesthesia, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or lower extremity weakness. He attributes some of this to his known spinal metastases. He has been taking acetaminophen 1000 mg three times daily with partial relief. He has not required opioids.

He denies right upper quadrant pain, early satiety, or significant weight loss (reports approximately 2 pounds weight loss over the past two months, which he attributes to decreased appetite from fatigue). He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or hemoptysis. He does report ongoing grade 1 fatigue and grade 1 hot flashes, which are longstanding and attributed to ADT; these have not significantly worsened.

He was found to carry a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) on comprehensive germline testing approximately 12 months ago. He has informed his adult children of the finding; one daughter has undergone cascade genetic counseling. Somatic tumor profiling via CT-guided biopsy of the L3 vertebral metastasis has been sent and results are pending at today's visit (biopsy was performed approximately 3 weeks ago). Preliminary read confirmed malignant adenocarcinoma consistent with prostatic primary.

Overall, the clinical picture is consistent with the development of castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) by established criteria: three consecutive PSA rises above nadir, all measured at least one week apart, with a PSA doubling time of approximately 3.5 months, in the setting of confirmed castrate testosterone levels (<10 ng/dL). This constitutes metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) in the context of known bone and nodal metastatic disease.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Low back pain (3/10, new onset, worse than prior stable level), fatigue (grade 1, chronic), hot flashes (grade 1, chronic/ADT-related), mild decrease in appetite (approximately 2 lb weight loss over 2 months).

Negative: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No chest pain or dyspnea. No cough or hemoptysis. No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. No abdominal pain, right upper quadrant discomfort, or jaundice. No hematuria, dysuria, or change in urinary stream. No neurological symptoms — specifically no lower extremity weakness, numbness, saddle anesthesia, or bowel/bladder incontinence. No new headaches, visual changes, or dizziness. No peripheral edema (historically monitored due to abiraterone). No fractures or falls.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, Heart rate 74 bpm (regular), Respiratory rate 16, Temperature 98.4°F oral, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 172 lbs (approximately 2 lb less than 3 months ago), Height 5'10", BMI 24.7.

General: Well-appearing, mildly fatigued-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Communicates clearly and is accompanied by his daughter today.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae pale-pink without frank pallor. Oropharynx clear, no mucosal lesions.

Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1, S2 normal. No S3 or S4. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema in bilateral lower extremities (notably reassuring given prior abiraterone use).

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, non-tender. No right upper quadrant tenderness or hepatomegaly appreciated on palpation. No splenomegaly. No palpable masses. Normoactive bowel sounds.

Musculoskeletal: Tenderness to palpation over the lumbar spine at L3-L4 level, consistent with known metastatic disease at these sites. No costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. No tenderness over the right iliac crest. Full range of motion of bilateral hips without pain (notably important given new hip symptoms will be monitored at next visit). No lower extremity bony tenderness.

Neurological: Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral lower extremities (hip flexion, knee extension, ankle dorsiflexion). Sensation grossly intact to light touch in bilateral lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric. Gait normal (non-antalgic). Romberg negative.

Lymph nodes: No cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy palpated.

Skin: No rash, jaundice, or bruising.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (TODAY)

PSA: 8.6 ng/mL (prior series: nadir 0.8 ng/mL → 1.1 → 2.4 → 5.3 → 8.6 ng/mL; PSA doubling time ~3.5 months)
Total Testosterone: <10 ng/dL (castrate range confirmed)
LH/FSH: Suppressed (consistent with LHRH agonist therapy)
CBC: WBC 5.8 K/uL (ANC 3.6 K/uL), Hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, Hematocrit 37.2%, Platelets 198 K/uL — within acceptable limits, mild anemia likely secondary to ADT (normocytic)
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Na 139, K 4.2 mEq/L (reassuring, no hypokalemia on abi/pred), BUN 18, Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m² by CKD-EPI), Glucose 98, ALT 28 U/L, AST 24 U/L, Total Bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Alkaline Phosphatase 112 U/L (mildly elevated; followed chronically in context of bone mets), Albumin 3.8 g/dL
LDH: 198 U/L (upper limit of normal)
Note: Cortisol not checked today; patient on prednisone 5 mg BID (per abiraterone protocol), being tapered off with abiraterone discontinuation discussed below.
Somatic tumor NGS panel (CT-guided L3 biopsy, 3 weeks prior): RESULTS PENDING — preliminary pathology confirmed adenocarcinoma of prostatic origin; full molecular results expected within 7-10 business days.

---

IMAGING RESULTS (RECENT)

Bone scintigraphy (Tc-99m) and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, performed approximately 6 months ago: Known lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala were stable with sclerotic changes consistent with a treated/healing pattern. Right external iliac lymph nodes had decreased to 0.9 cm. No visceral metastases identified at that time.

No new imaging has been performed since that 18-month scan. Given the current PSA trajectory and new back pain, repeat staging scans will be ordered as part of today's plan.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (mCRPC) — NEW DIAGNOSIS

Mr. Kowalski has developed castration-resistant prostate cancer by standard criteria (PCWGx): three rising PSA values above nadir (PSA now 8.6 ng/mL, up from nadir of 0.8 ng/mL), with confirmed castrate testosterone <10 ng/dL, representing failure of first-line intensified androgen deprivation therapy with abiraterone. This is not unexpected — the median progression-free survival for mHSPC on ADT plus abiraterone (LATITUDE, STAMPEDE data) is approximately 24 months, and he has now reached that landmark.

His germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (confirmed ~12 months ago) and the pending somatic tumor profile are critically relevant to next-line therapy selection. BRCA2 biallelic loss, if confirmed somatically (as is expected given the germline variant and typical tumor biology in this setting), renders him eligible for olaparib (Lynparza) per the PROfound trial FDA-approved indication. However, we will await the NGS results before finalizing next-line therapy.

Key considerations in treatment selection:
- Prior abiraterone use: Cross-resistance with enzalutamide exists to some degree, but sequential AR pathway inhibitor (ARPI) use is commonly employed in clinical practice. Given that his abiraterone therapy lasted 24 months with a prolonged PSA response, a further AR pathway-targeted approach with enzalutamide is reasonable.
- BRCA2 germline/somatic mutation: This is a tier 1 actionable alteration. PARP inhibitor (olaparib 300 mg BID) is FDA-approved in this setting (post-one ARPI), but preferring to sequence enzalutamide first is defensible and consistent with patient preference to defer chemotherapy-associated options; olaparib reserved as second-line within mCRPC.
- ECOG PS: Currently 1. Patient is a reasonable candidate for most available options.
- Renal function: eGFR 52 mL/min — relevant for agent selection and dose considerations going forward. Olaparib does not require dose adjustment at this GFR level.
- Patient preference: Reviewed treatment options thoroughly with Mr. Kowalski and his daughter. He understands the disease has progressed and is no longer being controlled by current therapy. He strongly prefers oral options and would like to defer intravenous chemotherapy for as long as possible. He is comfortable with continued monitoring and does not want to rush into aggressive treatment.

Plan:
- Discontinue abiraterone acetate 1000 mg daily (last dose today). Taper prednisone 5 mg BID — reduce to 5 mg daily for 1 week, then discontinue (to allow HPA axis recovery after prolonged low-dose corticosteroid exposure).
- Initiate enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg orally once daily (4 × 40 mg capsules). Reviewed key side effects: fatigue, hot flashes, hypertension, dizziness/fall risk, and the rare but important risk of seizure (<1%; patient counseled to avoid activities risking significant injury if seizure occurs). He has no prior seizure history, no history of traumatic brain injury, and takes no medications that lower seizure threshold. Instructed to take with or without food; avoid concomitant CYP enzyme inducers.
- Continue leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months to maintain castrate testosterone — next injection due in approximately 6 weeks (schedule confirmed with nursing).
- Baseline labs prior to enzalutamide initiation confirmed adequate today (CBC, CMP as above).
- Await somatic NGS results (expected within 7-10 days); results will be reviewed with patient at next visit and will inform sequencing of future therapy (PARP inhibitor consideration after enzalutamide).
- Repeat bone scan (Tc-99m whole body scintigraphy) and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis ordered to establish new mCRPC baseline — patient will complete these studies within the next 2 weeks.

2. LOW BACK PAIN — NEW, RELATED TO KNOWN SPINAL METASTASES

New low back pain at L3-L4 level is most consistent with known osseous metastatic disease at those sites in the setting of biochemical progression. Neurological examination is reassuring — full motor strength, intact sensation, no signs of cord compression.

- Continue acetaminophen 1000 mg TID scheduled (not to exceed 3 g/day; counseled to avoid additional OTC acetaminophen-containing products).
- Added oxycodone IR 5 mg every 4-6 hours as needed for breakthrough pain (not to exceed 4 doses per 24 hours); patient instructed on constipation prophylaxis (docusate sodium 100 mg BID, senna 8.6 mg BID) given opioid initiation.
- If pain worsens, or if any neurological symptoms develop (particularly lower extremity weakness, changes in bladder/bowel), patient instructed to go to the emergency department immediately given risk for epidural spinal cord compression (ESCC).
- Pain and spinal status will be reassessed at next visit. Radiation oncology referral for palliative radiation to lumbar spine will be considered if pain not adequately controlled with current measures or if neurological concern arises.
- Bone-modifying agent therapy: Given transition to mCRPC with known bone metastases, initiating a bone-modifying agent was discussed. Zoledronic acid (Zometa) would be standard but GFR of 52 mL/min warrants caution with bisphosphonates; denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks is the preferred option in this patient given his renal function. Will initiate denosumab at next visit once imaging is reviewed and mCRPC diagnosis is formally staged. Calcium 500 mg with vitamin D 400 IU daily supplementation instructed to begin today in anticipation.

3. GERMLINE BRCA2 — ONGOING MANAGEMENT

Patient carrying germline BRCA2 c.5946delT (heterozygous, pathogenic). Somatic biallelic loss is expected on the pending tumor NGS (second hit via LOH/somatic deletion). Family cascade testing is underway (one daughter has been seen in genetic counseling). No further germline action needed at this time.

4. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE, STAGE 3A (eGFR 52 mL/min)

Likely multifactorial (age, baseline, possible contrast exposure history). Stable from prior checks. Will continue to monitor creatinine monthly. Avoid nephrotoxic agents where possible. NSAIDs explicitly avoided.

5. ADT-RELATED EFFECTS

Patient has been on continuous ADT for approximately 24 months. He has baseline grade 1 fatigue and hot flashes. Anemia (Hgb 12.1 g/dL) is mild and consistent with ADT-related erythropoietic suppression. No osteoporosis-related fracture to date. DEXA scan should be ordered given 24 months of ADT — if osteopenia or osteoporosis identified, denosumab (planned above) will serve dual purpose. Patient counseled on fall precautions, calcium/vitamin D supplementation (initiated above).

FOLLOW-UP

Mr. Kowalski will follow up in 3 weeks to review: (1) somatic NGS tumor profiling results, (2) repeat bone scan and CT staging images, and (3) initial tolerability of enzalutamide. Prescription for enzalutamide provided to the specialty pharmacy; patient counseled that a prior authorization process may take 3-5 business days. He will call the triage nurse line if he experiences any new neurological symptoms, severe pain, falls, fever, or other urgent concerns prior to his next scheduled visit.

Patient and his daughter verbalized understanding of today's discussion including the diagnosis of castration-resistant prostate cancer, the transition in therapy, and the next steps. Questions were answered. He is coping reasonably well and remains engaged in his care. Palliative care has not yet been formally involved but will be considered in the near future as disease burden increases.

ECOG Performance Status: 1
Total visit time: 55 minutes (>50% of which was counseling and coordination of care).

— Electronically signed, Medical Oncology Attending
